sections.heading: What is the Repository Pattern?
sections.content: The Repository pattern is a design pattern that acts as an abstraction layer between the data sources of your app (local database, remote API, cache) and the rest of the application (ViewModel, use cases). Instead of the ViewModel directly calling Room DAOs or Retrofit API services, it calls the Repository — a single class that knows where data comes from and how to coordinate between multiple sources. The Repository is the single source of truth for a domain of data in your app, deciding whether to serve cached local data or fetch fresh data from the network.

sections.heading: Why Use the Repository Pattern?
items: Single source of truth — the Repository decides which data source to use and presents a unified data interface
Separation of concerns — ViewModel does not know or care whether data comes from Room, Retrofit, or a cache
Testability — swap the real Repository with a fake implementation in unit tests without changing ViewModel code
Maintainability — changing the data source (e.g., switching from REST to GraphQL) only affects the Repository
Offline support — Repository can serve cached local data when there is no network connectivity
Caching strategy — Repository implements cache-first, network-first, or cache-and-network logic in one place
Clean architecture alignment — Repository is the boundary between the data layer and the domain/presentation layer
Reusability — multiple ViewModels can use the same Repository without duplicating data access logic

sections.heading: Repository Pattern in the App Architecture

rows.interacts_with: ViewModel only — never directly with Repository or data sources
Repository only — does not know if data is local or remote
Local data sources (Room DAO) and remote data sources (Retrofit API)
Remote REST API over the network

sections.heading: Anatomy of a Repository Class
sections.content: A Repository is a plain Kotlin class with no Android framework dependencies. It receives its data sources via constructor injection (typically provided by Hilt) and exposes data through suspend functions or Flow streams. It contains the logic for deciding when to fetch from the network, when to read from the local database, and how to handle errors. The ViewModel calls the Repository's public API and has no knowledge of the underlying implementation details.

sections.heading: Repository Class Structure
items: Declare the class with @Inject constructor for Hilt compatibility: class UserRepository @Inject constructor(...)
Accept data sources as constructor parameters: private val userDao: UserDao, private val apiService: UserApiService
Expose read operations as Flow for reactive updates: fun getUsers(): Flow<List<User>> = userDao.getAllUsers()
Expose network operations as suspend functions: suspend fun refreshUsers() — called from ViewModel's viewModelScope
Wrap network calls in try-catch or use a Result wrapper to handle errors gracefully
Use withContext(Dispatchers.IO) for blocking I/O operations if not already on an IO dispatcher
Never expose internal data source types (Room entities, API DTOs) directly — map to domain models
Keep the Repository focused on one domain area: UserRepository for users, ProductRepository for products

rows.description: Always serve data from local database first; fetch from network in background to refresh
rows.best_for: Apps that need offline support; news, social feeds, e-commerce catalogs
rows.flow: Room → emit local data → fetch network → update Room → Room emits updated data

rows.description: Always fetch from network first; fall back to local cache only on failure
rows.best_for: Real-time data where freshness is critical; stock prices, live scores
rows.flow: Fetch network → success: update local + emit; failure: emit from Room cache

rows.description: Always fetch from network; no local caching
rows.best_for: Data that should never be stale; one-time operations, authentication
rows.flow: Fetch network → emit result; no Room involvement

rows.description: Only read and write local database; no network calls
rows.best_for: User-created content, notes, local settings, to-do items
rows.flow: Room → emit data; writes go directly to Room

rows.description: Emit local cache immediately while simultaneously fetching fresh network data
rows.best_for: Perceived performance — show something immediately, update when fresh data arrives
rows.flow: Emit Room data → fetch network in parallel → update Room → Room emits again

sections.heading: Repository with Flow — Reactive Data
sections.content: The most powerful pattern for a Repository is exposing data as a Kotlin Flow. When a Repository function returns Flow<List<User>> from a Room DAO, any change to the users table in the database automatically emits a new list through the Flow. The ViewModel collects this Flow and updates its StateFlow or LiveData. The View observes the ViewModel's state and re-renders. This creates a fully reactive data pipeline — a database write anywhere in the app automatically propagates all the way up to the UI without any manual refreshing.

sections.heading: Implementing Cache-First Repository
items: Room DAO returns Flow<List<User>> — any database change automatically emits a new list
Repository exposes the Flow directly: fun getUsers(): Flow<List<User>> = userDao.getAllUsers()
Repository also has a suspend refresh function: suspend fun refresh() that fetches from API and inserts into Room
ViewModel collects the Flow: viewModelScope.launch { repository.getUsers().collect { _uiState.value = it } }
ViewModel triggers refresh in init block: viewModelScope.launch { repository.refresh() }
After refresh() inserts new data into Room, the Flow from getUsers() automatically emits the updated list
The View observes the ViewModel's StateFlow and updates automatically — no manual pull-to-refresh logic needed
Handle refresh errors separately — the cached data Flow continues emitting even if the network refresh fails

rows.description: Represents the structure of JSON from the API — matches the API response exactly
rows.example: data class UserDto(val user_id: Int, val full_name: String, val email_address: String)

rows.description: Represents a database table row — annotated with @Entity for Room
rows.example: data class UserEntity(@PrimaryKey val id: Int, val name: String, val email: String)

rows.description: Clean, framework-free model used by the ViewModel and UI — no Room or Retrofit annotations
rows.example: data class User(val id: Int, val name: String, val email: String)

rows.description: Extension functions in the Repository or a dedicated mapper class that convert between model types
rows.example: fun UserDto.toEntity(): UserEntity = UserEntity(user_id, full_name, email_address)

sections.heading: Error Handling in the Repository
items: Wrap network calls in try-catch inside the Repository: try { apiService.getUsers() } catch (e: Exception) { ... }
Use a sealed Result wrapper class: sealed class Result<T> { data class Success<T>(val data: T); data class Error(val exception: Exception) }
Return Result<T> from suspend functions: suspend fun getUser(id: Int): Result<User>
In the ViewModel, handle Result: when (result) { is Result.Success -> ...; is Result.Error -> ... }
Use Kotlin's runCatching { } for concise try-catch: val result = runCatching { apiService.getUsers() }
For Flow-based repositories, use catch operator: flow.catch { e -> emit(emptyList()) }
Log errors in the Repository for debugging but expose user-friendly messages to the ViewModel
Never swallow exceptions silently — always propagate error state to the ViewModel and ultimately the UI

rows.sealed_class: data class Success<out T>(val data: T) : Result<T>()
rows.viewmodel_handling: Update UI state with the data: _uiState.value = UiState.Success(result.data)

rows.sealed_class: data class Error<T>(val exception: Throwable) : Result<T>()

sections.heading: Repository Interface vs Concrete Class
items: Define a Repository interface: interface UserRepository { fun getUsers(): Flow<List<User>>; suspend fun refresh() }
Create a concrete implementation: class UserRepositoryImpl @Inject constructor(...) : UserRepository
Bind the interface to the implementation in a Hilt module using @Binds
The ViewModel depends on the interface: class UserViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: UserRepository)
In tests, provide a FakeUserRepository that implements the same interface with predictable test data
This makes the ViewModel 100% testable — inject the fake, assert on StateFlow values
The interface approach is especially valuable in Clean Architecture where the domain layer defines the contract
For small apps, a concrete class without an interface is acceptable — add the interface when testability is needed

rows.description: interface UserRepository { fun getUsers(): Flow<List<User>>; suspend fun refresh() }
class UserRepositoryImpl @Inject constructor(private val dao: UserDao, private val api: UserApiService) : UserRepository
@Binds abstract fun bindUserRepository(impl: UserRepositoryImpl): UserRepository — in @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
@HiltViewModel class UserViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: UserRepository) : ViewModel()

sections.heading: Repository Best Practices
items: One Repository per domain feature — UserRepository, OrderRepository, ProductRepository — not one giant repository
Keep the Repository layer free of Android framework dependencies — it should be a plain Kotlin class
Always map between data models — never leak Room entities or Retrofit DTOs to the ViewModel or UI layer
Use Flow for data that changes over time (database queries) and suspend functions for one-shot operations (API calls)
Implement error handling in the Repository — the ViewModel should receive a clean Result or handle exceptions uniformly
Inject data sources via constructor — makes the Repository easily replaceable in tests
Avoid adding UI logic, context references, or ViewModel-specific logic in the Repository
Document the caching strategy in comments — future developers need to understand when data is local vs remote

sections.heading: Repository Pattern vs Direct Data Access

rows.with_repository: Low — ViewModel calls simple functions on Repository
rows.without_repository: High — ViewModel manages Room, Retrofit, caching, error handling directly

rows.with_repository: High — fake Repository injected in ViewModel tests
rows.without_repository: Low — must mock Room and Retrofit separately in every ViewModel test

rows.with_repository: Isolated — only Repository changes when switching data sources
rows.without_repository: Scattered — every ViewModel using the data source must be updated

rows.with_repository: Centralized in Repository — one place to maintain
rows.without_repository: Duplicated across every ViewModel that needs cached data

rows.with_repository: Easy — Repository handles fallback to local cache transparently
rows.without_repository: Complex — each ViewModel must implement its own offline fallback

rows.with_repository: None — all data access logic in one place per domain
rows.without_repository: High — same fetch, cache, error logic repeated in multiple ViewModels

sections.heading: Common Repository Mistakes to Avoid
items: Putting UI logic in the Repository — it should only handle data, not strings formatted for display or navigation
Returning Room entities or API DTOs directly — always map to domain models before exposing from the Repository
Creating a single massive AppRepository — split into domain-specific repositories for maintainability
Not handling network errors — always wrap API calls in try-catch and propagate errors via Result or sealed class
Blocking the main thread in the Repository — use suspend functions and Dispatchers.IO for all I/O operations
Storing Activity or Fragment context in the Repository — it is a non-Android class; use ApplicationContext only if needed
Forgetting to cancel ongoing operations — use viewModelScope in the ViewModel; coroutines are cancelled automatically
Exposing MutableStateFlow or MutableLiveData from Repository — only expose immutable Flow or read-only types

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: The Repository pattern is the cornerstone of a clean, scalable Android data layer. By creating a single abstraction that hides the complexity of coordinating between local databases, remote APIs, and caches, you give your ViewModels a simple, consistent interface to work with regardless of where data actually comes from. Combined with Hilt for dependency injection, Flow for reactive data streams, and a clear mapping strategy between data models, the Repository pattern makes your app offline-capable, thoroughly testable, and resilient to changes in underlying data sources.
